retrieved_docs.content: There are several options available with orthodontic appliances, including palatal cribs, spurs, palatal bars, hay rakes, and cage-type appliances. retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Osteotomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: LeFort I osteotomy is used to reposition the dentition-bearing maxilla and is indicated for the following conditions: Maxillomandibular deformities Maxillary hypoplasia or mandibular hyperplasia Vertical maxillary excess Angle’s class II and class III malocclusions Midline discrepancies or asymmetries Apertognathia or open bite Absolute transverse arch discrepancy* Dual or multiplanar occlusion* Severe maxillary atrophy, in conjunction with bone grafting Obstructive sleep apnea Access for skull base tumors Facilitation of reduction of nonreducible fractures [6] [7] [8] *May require segmental or multipiece LeFort osteotomy, which is not covered in this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: A class II division 2 is when the maxillary central incisors are palatally inclined and may be overlapped by the maxillary lateral incisors. A deep overbite and a broad maxillary arch define a class II division 2. There is a normal upper lip seal and a deep mental groove. Unlike division 1, division 2 has a normal-sized mandible. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are several methods to treat malocclusion. Palatal expanders are traditionally used before braces treatment to treat a narrow palate, crowding, or crossbite. Rapid palatal expansion (RPE) works by placing a force on the two maxillary bones to split the mid-palatal suture. Palatal expanders are most useful during adolescence, as the maxillary bones become fused during puberty. The expander contains a screw turned daily or as directed by the patient's orthodontist. Every turn places pressure on the maxillary bone, allowing for less than 1 mm of expansion. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Class I malocclusion is further categorized into three types by Dewey. In Dewey type 1, the incisors are crowded, the canines are positioned labially, or both. [5] In Dewey type 2, the maxillary incisors protrude. In type 3, the anterior teeth occlude edge to edge, or there is a crossbite or both. [5] The Anderson classification further categorizes class I malocclusion into types 4 and 5. In type 4, there is a posterior crossbite, which could be unilateral or bilateral. Type 5 occlusion is a class I molar relationship with mesioversion of the permanent first molar due to the extraction of a second deciduous molar or premolar. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Midface Hypoplasia : Midface hypoplasia can present as a result of several craniofacial syndromes (Treacher Collins, Cohen syndrome, etc.) and in patients with isolated cleft lip or palate. Patients will often have visible malar retrusion, class 3 malocclusion, and a degree of exorbitism due to isolated retrodisplacement of the inferior orbital rim. Similar to mandibular hypoplasia, patients may present with airway obstruction or severe sleep apnea. Distraction osteogenesis has proven a useful tool in correcting these abnormalities. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Around 32% of individuals with malocclusion have a class II. A class II molar relationship is when the mandible is positioned retrognathic to the maxilla. Class II interarch relationship is categorized into two divisions. A Class II division 1 is when the maxillary incisors are protruded with an excessive overjet and deep overbite. The maxillary arch is often v-shaped and narrow in the canine region and broad between molar regions. Patients with a class II molar relationship division 1 have a shorter upper lip and often fail to close their anterior lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Retrognathia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment and management will depend on what the clinician's concerns are and what a patient or their parents desire. Most commonly retrognathia will be identified in children between the ages of four to ten years old and if the child has a dental home, will likely be referred to an orthodontist for evaluation ideally around the ages of seven to eight. Generally, this is a benign condition without any other effects. This will allow the orthodontist to begin tracking the patient with serial cephalometric radiographs and potentially intervene early with non-surgical therapy like headgear or newer intra-oral techniques that have a similar effect in repositioning the mandible. If the child does not have a dental home or has not seen an orthodontist, a referral is recommended. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: In Ackerman and Profitt's classification system, malocclusion is divided into classes I, II, III, IV, V, and VI. These categories are based on the relationship between the maxillary and mandibular teeth and the jaw position. Class I: The maxillary teeth are slightly forward of the mandibular teeth, and the jaw is aligned properly. Class II: The maxillary teeth are significantly forward of the mandibular teeth, and the jaw is underdeveloped. Class III: The mandibular teeth are significantly forward of the maxillary teeth, and the jaw is overdeveloped. Class IV: The maxillary teeth are significantly behind the mandibular teeth. Class V: The maxillary teeth are significantly forward of the mandibular teeth, and the jaw is overdeveloped. Class VI: The mandibular teeth are significantly behind the maxillary teeth. [9]

Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are both classified under the broader concept of uterine function. Within this uterine function category, two additional conditions are identified: الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation) and عطالة رحمية ثانوية (secondary uterine inertia). Together, these entities represent a spectrum of uterine functional abnormalities that can influence the course of labor and delivery, highlighting the interrelated nature of fetal head positioning anomalies and complications such as premature placental detachment and reduced uterine contractility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.
